event_blocks.summary: At the 100 Metres Decathlon of the World Championships Oregon 22, amidst crowd noise and a commentator's voice, athletes lined up on the track, beginning with Canada's Pierce Lepage in lane 1, adjusting his stance in a red and white striped singlet, with the commentator noting his past DNF and fifth-place finishes. Kevin Mayer of France in lane 2, wearing a white and blue singlet, placed a hand on his chest and waved, as the commentator highlighted his two Olympic silvers. Damian Warner of Canada, in lane 3 with a red and black striped singlet, raised his arm in acknowledgment, praised by the commentator for his "season senior performer" status and Olympic champion title with a 9,000 points score. Zachary Ziemek of the USA, in lane 4 and a red and blue striped singlet, waved to the audience. Lindon Victor of Grenada, in lane 5 and a green and red singlet, raised his arm in a celebratory motion, identified as the Commonwealth Games champion with top 10 World Champs finishes. Ashley Moloney of Australia, in lane 6 and a green and yellow singlet, clapped his hands and looked forward, with the commentator recalling his surprise Tokyo bronze and questioning his ability to replicate it. Lastly, Garland of the USA, in lane 7 and a red singlet, stood composed with hands on hips, as the commentator began his introduction. The background showed a partially filled stadium with waving Canadian flags and TDK and QNB banners, while on-screen graphics displayed athlete information.

event_blocks.summary: On the red track of the World Athletics Championships Oregon 22, Damian Warner of Canada, in lane 3, demonstrated exceptional speed in the final stretch of the 100 metres decathlon, leading the pack with Lepage (Canada, lane 1) and Moloney (Australia, lane 6) close behind, the latter having initially pressured Warner. Warner secured first place with an unofficial time of 10.28 seconds under a +0.8 m/s wind, followed by Lepage in second and Moloney in third, as confirmed by on-screen graphics. Post-race, a visibly pleased Warner acknowledged the crowd and competitors, with commentators highlighting his grit, determination, and conversion of fitness into titles, referencing a past 'messy' hurdles event. Lepage, in a red and white striped singlet, maintained a composed demeanor, while Moloney, in green and yellow, recovered by bending over. Other athletes, including Ziemek (USA), Victor (Grenada), and Owens-Delermé (Puerto Rico), were seen interacting and recovering amidst a charged atmosphere in the spectator-filled stadium adorned with 'Oregon 22' and 'World Athletics Championships' banners. Commentators further lauded Warner's speed and versatility across events like the long jump, 400m, and 110m hurdles, stating that speed is brilliant.

event_blocks.summary: The segment focuses on Canadian athlete Lepage during the decathlon long jump at the World Athletics Championships Oregon22. event_blocks.summary: During the World Athletics Championships Oregon 22, Kevin Mayer of France, in a white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet, celebrated a 7.51m long jump, with commentators noting his increasing happiness and usual medal contention. Zachary Ziemek of the USA, in a red and blue striped 'USA' singlet, executed a powerful shot put throw, achieving 15.28m and placing second, a performance commentators deemed a personal best with potential for more. The bright, sunny stadium, adorned with 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' banners and filled with spectators in green and purple stands, buzzed with competitive energy, overseen by officials in white. Later, Ziemek, identified as 'Double Z,' performed a precise high jump, soaring over 208 and earning 878 points, described as being "on fire." Kevin Mayer, the "world record holder," cleared 205 in the high jump with a smooth motion, showing no signs of ankle trouble and poised to impact the leaderboard, as evidenced by a bar clearance graphic showing a clean 1.9 cm clearance. Earlier, Pierce Lepage of Canada, in a red and white striped 'CANADA' singlet and a black knee brace on his right leg, after struggling with earlier shot put throws of 14.26m and 14.29m, managed a crucial 14.83m throw, placing him 9th in Group A, before transitioning to the high jump where he cleared the bar at 199, described as a "big Canadian delivering."

The segment opens with American athlete Ziemek, in a red and blue striped 'USA' singlet, executing a powerful shot put throw under bright sunlight. Commentators identify him as Zach Ziemek, noting a 'personal best' and mentioning a throw of '15.37' meters, though the scoreboard updates to show 15.28m, placing him third in Group A, with commentators remarking on 'two personal bests' and the pressure on other athletes. The atmosphere is competitive, with spectators in green and purple stands and a loud 'AHHH!' heard during a throw, which commentators describe as 'fantastic for fans.' The scene transitions to French athlete Mayer, in a white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet, preparing for his shot put attempt. Commentators refer to him as 'the world record holder' and identify him as Kevin Mayer, confirming his throw of '14 meters and 98' but immediately noting he 'fouled' by going 'past the toe board,' describing it as an 'intentional foul.' They observe a lack of 'buoyancy and positivity' in him compared to previous events. The camera then cuts to Canadian athlete Lepage, in a red and white striped 'CANADA' singlet, stretching and appearing focused. The stadium features 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' banners, and officials in white uniforms oversee the event, while commentators discuss the importance of athletes putting 'failures behind you.'

The video opens with Canadian athlete Pierce Lepage, wearing a red and white striped 'CANADA' singlet, celebrating a shot put attempt by raising his arms in satisfaction, as a male commentator discusses the importance of learning from past attempts and moving on in multi-event sports. The camera then cuts to a wide-angle view of the shot put ball landing in the sandpit, observed by officials and photographers, before Lepage, appearing composed, returns for another powerful rotational throw, spinning with focused intensity and exiting dynamically with arms raised in triumph. A female commentator specifically details his performance, noting his earlier "pretty naff" throws of 14.26m and 14.29m displayed on the scoreboard, but praising his "good composure" for "managing to turn it around" with a strong 14.83m throw, which she emphasizes is crucial as 14.29m would have been a "big disaster." The scoreboard confirms his results, placing him 9th in Group A. After the throw, Lepage stretches, visibly tired but composed, preparing for the next event. The scene transitions to the high jump decathlon, where Lepage walks along the track, wearing a knee brace on his right leg, appearing focused and deliberate. Another male commentator briefly mentions Lepage by name, referencing his past battles for the podium in Doha and Tokyo, as the bright, sunny stadium with green and purple seating and 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' banners sets a competitive yet calm atmosphere.

The segment opens at the World Athletics Championships Oregon 22 with Canadian athlete Lepage, in his red and white 'CANADA' singlet and a black knee brace on his right leg, preparing for his high jump. Commentators note he's "more than capable at 199" as he executes a powerful run-up, clearing the bar by "10 centimeters higher" without even a wobble, a "big Canadian delivering." The scene transitions to American athlete Ziemek, in his red and blue 'USA' singlet, introduced as "Double Z." He performs a precise high jump, soaring over 208, which commentators highlight as a "brilliant competition" jump earning him 878 points, declaring him "on fire in this event" despite only three jumps. Next, French athlete Mayer, the "world record holder" in his white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet, is seen mid-run, then clearing the bar at 205 with a smooth, controlled motion. A dramatic low-angle camera shot emphasizes his form. Commentators praise "Well done Kevin Mayer," noting he's "showing no signs of that problem with the right ankle" and recalling his past struggles in Doha, but affirming his potential to "shake up the leaderboard." The segment concludes with a bar clearance graphic showing a clean clearance of 1.9 cm at the top and 0.0 cm at the closest point, all under bright daylight with spectators in green and purple stands and event banners visible.

event_blocks.summary: The 400-meter decathlon race at the World Athletics Championships Oregon 22 commenced with intense focus from athletes like Uibo, Mullings, Ziemek, and Nowak. Following a starting gun, the runners surged forward, with live tracking graphics illustrating their positions. Õiglane took an early lead, with Ziemek, Nowak, and Mullings in close pursuit. As the race progressed, Õiglane maintained his lead, followed by Ziemek, Nowak, and Mullings, with the commentator noting the physical toll and the crowd's energy. In the final moments, Õiglane crossed the finish line first, collapsing in exhaustion, followed closely by Mullings and Ziemek. Later, Kaul, the defending champion, finished first in his heat, with Kazmirek and Victor also performing strongly, though Kazmirek was noted to have started too fast. The race concluded with athletes like Lepage and Moloney demonstrating significant fatigue, with Lepage wearing a knee brace. Throughout the event, commentators highlighted athletes' performances, physical states, and strategic approaches, emphasizing the grueling nature of the decathlon, with the stadium filled with spectators and prominent event branding.

Õiglane.description: Estonian athlete in a blue and white striped 'ESTONIA' singlet with 'TDK' and 'OREGON22' branding, wearing number 8, who wins the 400m decathlon with a time of 49.18 seconds, collapsing on the track in exhaustion before slowly sitting up, visibly fatigued and catching his breath. Commentators note that 'Õiglane's legs were beginning to go' but he 'did take the win' and was 'really feeling it'.

Group B High Jump Decathlon Scoreboard.description: On-screen scoreboard showing Mayer leading with 2.05m, followed by Kaul, Õiglane, and Warner all at 2.02m, with other athletes listed below, including Neugebauer at 1.99m and Colubovic at 1.96m.

Uibo.description: Estonian athlete in a blue and white striped 'ESTONIA' singlet with 'TDK' and 'OREGON22' branding, identified by the commentator as 'Uibo' and a 'Silver medalist three years ago,' initially seen focused with hands on hips, then assuming the starting position in the blocks in lane 8, and later noted by the commentator as being 'on the outside for Estonia' and observed 'sitting in the front row right there on the first turn' of the 400m decathlon. His on-screen stats show a season best of 51.08, personal best of 50.18, and world ranking of 31.

400 Metres Decathlon Track.description: Red running track with white lane markings, curving around the stadium, where athletes are seen recovering after the race, with lane numbers and country codes visible on the trackside.

Mayer.description: French athlete Kevin Mayer, in a white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet with 'MAYER' and 'TDK' branding, walks away from the finish line, catching his breath, and looking around the stadium. Commentators identify him by his full name, describe him as the 'defending champion from 2019,' and explain he 'ran conservatively' as it was his first 400 meters of the year.

Event Banner.description: Purple banners with 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' and colorful logos, visible along the trackside, reinforcing the event’s official branding and location.

Bar Clearance Graphic.description: On-screen graphic displaying 'BAR CLEARANCE' with 'TOP 1.9 CM' and 'CLOSEST 0.0 CM', indicating a successful high jump clearance by Mayer.

Starting Lineup - 400 Metres 1/3 Graphic.description: On-screen graphic listing the 400m decathlon starting lineup, including Õiglane, Uibo, Nowak, Ziemek, Mullings, and others, with their season bests, personal bests, and world rankings displayed.

Decathlon Scoreboard.description: On-screen graphic displaying 'DECATHLON' with 'WDB USA EATON 45.00' and 'CDB USA EATON 45.00', indicating previous results from the decathlon events.

Stadium Crowd.description: Spectators seated in green and purple stands, visible in the background, watching the athletes recover, adding to the atmosphere of a major championship event.

Mullings.description: Bahamian athlete in a turquoise 'BAHAMAS' singlet with 'MULLINGS' and 'OREGON22' branding, wearing number 5, who finishes second, standing and catching his breath, looking up with a mix of relief and fatigue. Commentators state he 'finished really quickly' and was the 'Bahamian' whom Õiglane was '900ths ahead of'.

Nowak.description: German athlete in a gold 'GERMANY' singlet with 'NOWAK' and 'TDK' branding, wearing number 2, who finishes third, seen crouched on the track, recovering from the race, with his hands on his knees.

Ziemek.description: American athlete in a red and blue striped 'USA' singlet with 'ZIEMEK' and 'TDK' branding, seen walking along the track after the 400m decathlon, appearing composed and interacting with a track official. Commentators refer to him as the '29-year-old from Wisconsin' who 'got off to a great start' and 'lost a little bit of ground towards the end'.

Seiko Timing Equipment.description: Yellow and black Seiko-branded timing devices positioned at the finish line, used to record the athletes' race times, with digital displays showing the final time of 48.40 seconds.

Live Tracking Graphic.description: On-screen graphic displaying real-time race data, including current positions (Kaul 1st, Kazmirek 2nd, Victor 4th) and distances to lead, updating as the race progresses.

Trackside Officials.description: Officials in white uniforms and caps, seen standing along the trackside near the finish line, overseeing the race and managing the timing equipment.

Race Timer.description: On-screen digital timer counting up from 0.0 to 48.40 seconds, tracking the elapsed time of the 400m decathlon race, visible in the bottom right corner of the screen.

200m Time Display.description: On-screen graphic showing 200m split times for the 400m decathlon, listing Õiglane (22.98), Ziemek (23.17), Nowak (23.55), and Mullings (23.64).

Unofficial Results Display.description: On-screen graphic showing the 400 metres decathlon results: Kaul in first, Kazmirek in second (+0.86), and Victor in third (+0.95), with flags and names clearly displayed, and a race timer at 48.40 seconds, which is also referenced by commentators.

Track Official.description: A track official in a white uniform and cap, standing near the trackside, overseeing the race and interacting with Ziemek after the event.

Tattooed Runner.description: Runner with a visible tattoo on his calf and a black bracelet inscribed 'Low Life', shown in a close-up of his lower leg as he prepares for the race.

Kazmirek.description: German athlete Kazmirek, in a white and blue singlet with 'GER' and 'KAZMIREK' on his bib, named by the announcer who notes he 'gone off really quickly' and 'may have gone too hard too early,' shown in the live tracking graphic as second place, running with strong form and visible effort.

Victor.description: Grenadian athlete Lyndon Victor, in a green and red singlet with 'GRENADA' and 'VICTOR' branding, is seen crouching on the track, resting with hand on chin, then looking toward the camera with a contemplative expression. Commentators identify him by his full name, praise his 'good finish' and 'stamina,' noting his time of 49.27 seconds and that he out-dipped competitors Secora and Kazmarek.

Black Athlete in Pink Singlet.description: Black athlete in a pink and white striped singlet with a gold chain and earring, seen bending forward, hands on knees, catching his breath before the race, with a focused expression.

Kaul.description: German athlete Nicholas Kaul, wearing a yellow and black striped singlet with 'GER' and 'KAUL' on his bib, is shown collapsing to the track in exhaustion, lying flat with arms and legs spread wide, visibly drained after the 400m decathlon. Commentators identify him by his full name, discuss his improving performance, and confirm his time of 48.39 seconds, which earns him over 890 points.

Lepage.description: Canadian athlete in a red and white striped 'CANADA' singlet with 'LEPAGE' and 'TDK' branding, seen walking off the track after the 400m decathlon, appearing fatigued but composed, with a black knee brace on his right leg. The commentator identifies him in lane 4, noting his 100m time of 10.39 and speculating if he could get close to 47.5 seconds for this season's best.

Starting Lineup - 400 Metres 3/3 Graphic.description: On-screen graphic listing the 400m decathlon starting lineup, including Warner, Bastien, Moloney, Lepage, Dubler, Neugebauer, Owens-Delermé, and Erm, with their season bests, personal bests, and world rankings displayed.

Moloney.description: Australian athlete in lane 6, wearing a green and yellow singlet with 'AUSTRALIA' and 'MOLONEY', bending over to catch his breath after finishing third, then standing up and walking off the track, visibly exhausted. The commentator refers to him as 'Ash Maloney' and mentions his bronze medal status.

raw_segments: The video opens dramatically with French athlete Mayer, in his white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet with 'adidas' and 'TDK' branding, successfully clearing the high jump bar with a smooth, controlled motion. An on-screen 'BAR CLEARANCE' graphic confirms the successful jump with 'TOP 1.9 CM' and 'CLOSEST 0.0 CM'. As he lands on the colorful mat, rolls, and stands up, walking away composed, commentators praise his performance, noting he's "really, really fired up tonight" and that his 2.05m jump, which leads Group B High Jump Decathlon on the scoreboard, is particularly impressive as he "hasn't high jumped this season" and it's only "4 centimeters below his all-time personal best." The scene transitions to a wide-angle view of the stadium, showing the 400m decathlon event in progress with athletes running on the red track under a bright blue sky. The camera then focuses on the starting line, where Estonian athletes Õiglane and Uibo, both in blue and white striped 'ESTONIA' singlets with 'TDK' branding, are seen preparing for the race. Õiglane waves to the electric crowd in the green and purple stands, while Uibo stands with hands on hips, both displaying focused expressions. An on-screen 'STARTING LINEUP - 400 METRES 1/3' graphic lists the competitors with their season bests and world rankings, as event banners for the 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' line the track. Amidst the crowd's cheers, a commentator announces "Õiglane!" noting he's "on the outside in the first of the heats," setting the stage for what is described as "tough going" in the 400m.

The video opens with a close-up of Estonian athlete Uibo, wearing a blue and white striped 'ESTONIA' singlet, appearing focused at the starting line, with the commentator identifying him as "Uibo" and a "Silver medalist three years ago." On-screen graphics display his season best (51.08), personal best (50.18), and world ranking (31). The camera then pans to Bahamian athlete Mullings in a turquoise 'BAHAMAS' singlet, who adjusts his headband and stretches, as the commentator identifies him as "Ken Mullings in five" from "the Bahamas," noting that lane six is empty due to Marcus Nilsson of Sweden not starting. Mullings' stats (SB 49.83, PB 49.18, WR 55) are shown. Next, American athlete Ziemek, in a red and blue striped 'USA' singlet, is seen stretching and pointing, with the commentator introducing him as "Zach Ziemek" from "the United States of America" in lane four, adding that he was "sixth in Tokyo" and had "really good performances" in the high jump. Ziemek's stats (SB 50.44, PB 49.04, WR 7) are displayed. The camera then focuses on German athlete Nowak, in a gold 'GERMANY' singlet, raising his arms and stretching, as the commentator identifies him as "Tim Nowak" from "Germany" in lane two, mentioning "He won a three men's 400 meters." Nowak's stats (SB 50.04, PB 48.90, WR 30) are shown. The commentator also notes that lane three is empty, mentioning Preciado of Ecuador who "got out the blocks in the 100 and then stops." The scene transitions to a wide aerial view of the 400m track, showing all athletes in their lanes, with lane numbers and country codes (GER, ECU, USA, BAH, SWE, EST) displayed trackside. The commentator confirms the positions: "Nowak's on the inside, Ziemek is in four, Mullings in five," and that "Uibo and Oiglane on the outside for Estonia." The camera then zooms in on Uibo in lane 8, who assumes the starting position in the blocks, hands on the track, legs bent, preparing for the race, with the '400 METRES DECATHLON' graphic visible. The atmosphere is intense, with a packed stadium crowd visible in the background, and 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' banners lining the track under bright daylight conditions, accompanied by continuous crowd murmurs and the commentator's ongoing analysis, including an observation about Uibo's position on the first turn.

The video opens with close-up shots of athletes in the starting blocks for the 400 metres decathlon, their intense concentration and physical readiness evident. A male commentator introduces Ken Mullings, noting his seasonal best of 49.83 seconds, and Janek Õiglane, with a seasonal best of 49.28 seconds, highlighting them as the two fastest in this heat. One athlete, Õiglane, wearing a blue and white striped singlet, is seen in a low crouch, while Ziemek, in a red and blue striped singlet, prepares with focused determination. Mullings, in a turquoise and yellow singlet, grips the track. A sharp starting gun fires, and the camera pulls back to a wide aerial view of the stadium, revealing the full red running track with white lane markings, curving around the field, and the packed green and purple stands filled with spectators. The athletes surge forward, with live tracking graphics appearing on screen showing current speeds and positions for USA, EST, and BAH. The commentator observes Mullings, the Bahamian, coming under pressure from Ziemek, while Õiglane, on the outside, is noted to be going well. He identifies Õiglane as a silver medalist who reclaimed some decent points in the high jump. As the runners navigate the first curve and continue along the straightaway, the crowd's roar is heard, specifically for Ziemek as they round the second bend. The scoreboard updates, showing Õiglane in first place at 22.98 seconds, followed by Ziemek, Nowak, and Mullings. The commentator confirms Õiglane is leading, then notes good running by Nowak on the inside, trying to get up onto the shoulder of Mullings, who is now responding as Ziemek begins to feel the pressure. The camera angle shifts to a side view, highlighting the runners' powerful strides and the crowd's energy, with banners for 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' visible along the trackside as the timer advances past 35 seconds, capturing the intense athleticism of the decathlon event.

The intense final moments of the 400 metres decathlon at the World Athletics Championships Oregon 22 conclude as Estonian athlete Õiglane, wearing number 8, crosses the finish line first. A commentator notes the 'effects of the lactic acid' and that 'Õiglane is slowing down' as he collapses onto the track, lying on his back in exhaustion, clutching his chest and gasping for air, clearly spent. He slowly sits up, still breathing heavily, while the unofficial results graphic confirms his victory with a time of 49.18 seconds, earning '853 points.' Bahamian athlete Ken Mullings, in a turquoise 'BAHAMAS' singlet, finishes closely behind, with the commentator noting he was 'finishing the fastest but he's second to Õiglane,' and his time is later confirmed as 49.25 seconds. Mullings is shown standing and catching his breath, looking up with a mix of relief and fatigue. German athlete Nowak, in a gold 'GERMANY' singlet, is seen crouched on the track, also recovering. The commentator confirms 'Ziemek is in third,' and later refers to him as Zach Ziemek, discussing how athletes 'listen to the crowd' to 'take that wave of energy' when 'absolutely exhausted.' The stadium crowd, visible in green and purple stands, watches intently, adding to the charged atmosphere. The red running track is marked with white lane lines, and Seiko timing equipment at the finish line displays Õiglane's winning time. Prominent purple banners along the trackside reinforce the 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' setting, as the camera captures the athletes' physical and emotional states after the grueling race.

The video opens with American athlete Ziemek, wearing a red and blue striped 'USA' singlet, walking along the track after the 400m decathlon race, appearing composed as he interacts with a track official. A commentator notes that 'he,' the 29-year-old from Wisconsin, 'got off to a great start' and had 'a good day all around.' The scene transitions to a wide-angle view of the stadium, showing the red running track curving around the field with spectators in green and purple stands. As athletes sprint, another commentator describes the grueling nature of the race, stating that after the 300-meter mark, 'it's all just guts,' and the last 50-60 meters are 'pure guts.' Estonian athlete Õiglane, in a blue and white striped 'ESTONIA' singlet with number 8, takes the lead with powerful strides and a focused expression. A commentator observes that 'Õiglane's legs were beginning to go here,' but he still crosses the finish line first, collapsing onto the track in exhaustion before slowly sitting up, visibly fatigued, having 'did take the win' and 'really feeling it.' Bahamian athlete Mullings, in a turquoise 'BAHAMAS' singlet, finishes second, standing and catching his breath with a mix of relief and fatigue, with the commentator noting Mullings 'finished really quickly' and Õiglane was '900ths ahead of the Bahamian.' German athlete Nowak, in a gold 'GERMANY' singlet, finishes third, crouched on the track recovering. On-screen graphics display 200m split times (Õiglane at 22.98, Ziemek at 23.17, Nowak at 23.55, Mullings at 23.64), while the race timer counts up to 42.4 seconds, and Seiko timing equipment at the finish line records the final time of 49.18 seconds. The stadium is electric with engaged and cheering spectators, and 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' banners are visible. A commentator adds that Ziemek 'lost a little bit of ground towards the end,' concluding with a confirmation of 'no disqualification.'

The video opens with a close-up of a runner’s tattooed calf and ankle, wearing a black bracelet inscribed 'Low Life,' as he prepares for the 400 metres decathlon. The camera then shifts to a Black athlete in a pink and white striped singlet, bending forward, hands on knees, catching his breath, with a gold chain and earring visible. An announcer explains that multi-eventers are allowed one false start before listing the competitors: 'Golubovic, Sekora, Mayer, Kaul, Kazmirek, Victor, Skoitham, Garland.' A wide aerial view shows multiple athletes in starting blocks on the red track, with lane numbers and officials visible. A loud starting gun signals the race's explosive beginning, and the camera follows the runners from a high angle, capturing their start and the crowd’s energy. The announcer highlights Kevin Mayer, 'third from the outside in that distinctive white and navy blue,' and notes that Garland is coming up onto his shoulder, with Kaul trying to 'pull himself back up.' Kazmirek is observed to have 'gone off really quickly,' passing Skoitham. As the runners navigate the curved track, on-screen graphics display split times (e.g., 15.6, 23.8 seconds) and live tracking. The camera zooms in on Kaul, the German athlete in a yellow and black striped singlet, who appears focused and determined. The live tracking graphic updates, showing Kaul in first, Kazmirek in second, and Lyndon Victor in fourth. The announcer comments on Mayer's 'heavily strapped' ankle and suggests Kazmirek 'may have gone too hard too early,' while Victor attempts to follow Kaul. As the runners approach the final stretch, the timer reaches 48.40 seconds. The video ends with a close-up of Kaul, the 'defending champion,' who has just finished, looking exhausted but composed, with the unofficial results graphic showing him in first place, followed by Kazmirek and Victor. The stadium is filled with spectators, and banners for 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' and sponsors are visible along the trackside.

The video captures the immediate aftermath of the 400 metres decathlon race on the red track, with commentators identifying athletes like "Lyndon Victor, Secora and Mayer all crossing the line pretty close together for decent points." The on-screen graphics display '400 METRES DECATHLON' and a race timer at 48.40 seconds. A close-up shows the German athlete, Nicholas Kaul, in his yellow and black striped singlet, visibly fatigued, as commentators remark, "I think there are signs here that Kaul... is getting better. And this is an improvement on what we've seen in previous seasons," confirming his time of "48.39" which will bring him "over 890 points." Kaul then collapses to the track in a full-body stretch, lying flat with arms and legs spread wide. Nearby, the Grenadian athlete, Lyndon Victor, in his green and red singlet, crouches on the track, resting with his hand on his chin, displaying a mix of exhaustion and contemplation. Commentators praise his "good finish" and "stamina, dragging that big strong frame around to 49.27," noting he "out-dipping Secora and Kazmarek on the line." Victor then shifts his gaze toward the camera, appearing to speak or react to the moment. The unofficial results display shows Kaul in first, Kazmirek in second, and Victor in third. The French athlete, Kevin Mayer, in his white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet, walks away from the finish line, catching his breath and looking around the stadium. Commentators describe Mayer as "the defending champion from 2019" and explain that he "ran conservatively but being his first 400 meters of the year, not surprising." The background reveals a packed stadium with spectators in green and purple stands, and 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' banners, all contributing to an atmosphere of intense physical exertion and post-race recovery.

The video captures a dynamic 400-meter decathlon race at the World Athletics Championships Oregon22, viewed from a high-angle perspective that follows the athletes as they sprint around the red track. Commentary describes the challenging final 100 meters, noting how athletes manage the pain better than expected. The race begins with a tight pack of runners, including Moloney (Australia, lane 6, green and yellow singlet) and Lepage (Canada, lane 4, red and white striped singlet), all displaying intense focus and powerful strides. As the runners navigate the curve, the camera pans smoothly to keep them in frame, highlighting their form and the competitive tension. The commentator highlights a "good finish" by the Germans and Kai Kazmirek's efforts to regain form, then praises Linden Victor's strong performance, stating he has "nothing to hang his head about" and "came through really well." The race concludes with a close finish, and the camera zooms in on the athletes as they slow down, catching their breath. Lepage is shown walking off the track, visibly fatigued but composed, with a black knee brace on his right leg, while Moloney bends over to recover, his hands on his knees, visibly exhausted. The on-screen graphic displays the starting lineup for the 400m decathlon, listing athletes such as Warner, Bastien, Moloney, Lepage, Dubler, Neugebauer, Owens-Delermé, and Erm, along with their season bests, personal bests, and world rankings. The commentator introduces Lepage in lane 4, mentioning his 100m time of 10.39 and wondering if he could get close to 47.5 seconds for this season's best, before identifying Moloney as "Ash Maloney," the bronze medalist. The stadium is packed with spectators in green and purple stands, adding to the electric atmosphere. The trackside features prominent branding from TDK, Seiko, QNB, and Asics, with digital timers and scoreboards visible, including a Seiko timing device at the finish line. The lighting is bright and natural, indicating a daytime event under clear skies, with the modern stadium architecture and large digital screens enhancing the professional setting.

event_blocks.summary: The 400 metres decathlon commenced with athletes including Australian Moloney, American Bastien, and Olympic champion Canadian Warner, who was leading after four events, taking their positions on the red track under bright daytime lighting and 'WCH OREGON 22' banners. The starting lineup graphic confirmed Warner in lane one, Bastien in two, Moloney in three, and Owens-Delermé, a Puerto Rican runner in a pink and white singlet highlighted as a pace-pusher, in lane seven. As the race unfolded, the stadium crowd cheered loudly, with Canadian fans waving flags for Warner. Owens-Delermé, described as having 'gone off like lightning' and tying Ashton Eaton's NCAA record, took the lead. Despite Warner's 'race is run' and Erm getting tired, Lepage began closing down the leaders. Owens-Delermé crossed the finish line first in 45.09 seconds, raising his arms in triumph, followed by Moloney and Lepage according to unofficial results. Owens-Delermé celebrated exuberantly, while a visibly exhausted Lepage, wearing a black knee brace on his right leg, sat on the track, receiving comfort from a female support staff member, a scene described as 'absolute tragedy' by commentators. The announcer detailed Owens-Delermé's impressive 1055 points for his finish, acknowledging the headwind. Trackside branding included Seiko and TDK.

Trackside Officials.description: Officials in white uniforms and caps, seen standing along the trackside near the finish line, overseeing the race and managing the timing equipment.

Bastien.description: American athlete Steven Bastien, in a red and blue striped 'USA' singlet with 'BASTIEN' and 'TDK' branding, is seen stretching and preparing for the 400m decathlon with a focused and determined demeanor; commentators introduce him as a 'late call up' from the USA, who placed fourth in the national championships and is hoping to achieve a personal best, later confirmed to be running in lane two.

400 Metres Decathlon Track.description: Red running track with white lane markings, curving around the stadium, where athletes are seen preparing and beginning the race, with lane numbers and country codes (CAN, USA, AUS, etc.) clearly marked on the trackside.

Event Banner.description: Purple banners with 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' and colorful logos, visible along the trackside, reinforcing the event’s official branding and location.

Starting Lineup - 400 Metres 3/3 Graphic.description: An on-screen graphic displays the 'STARTING LINEUP - 400 METRES 3/3', listing athletes like Warner, Bastien, Moloney, Lepage, Dubler, Neugebauer, Owens-Delermé, and Erm, with their season bests, personal bests, and world rankings displayed, which is verbally reinforced by commentators announcing each athlete's name and lane number.

Canadian Fan.description: An enthusiastic Canadian fan, wearing a red cap with a maple leaf, is seen waving a Canadian flag in the stands and cheering, showing strong national support for Warner and contributing to the audible crowd cheers.

Warner.description: Canadian athlete Warner, in a red, white, and black striped 'CANADA' singlet with 'WARNER' and 'TDK' branding, is shown with a determined look and later crouching in the starting blocks, hands on the track, eyes focused ahead. Commentators identify him as the Olympic champion, a bronze medalist in Moscow and Doha, and a silver medalist in Beijing, currently leading after four events with a season's best of 47.9 and a personal best of 46.5, running in lane one.

Moloney.description: Australian athlete in a green and yellow 'AUSTRALIA' singlet with 'MOLONEY' and 'TDK' branding, finishing second, slowing down with a composed demeanor, and looking toward the finish line. Commentators refer to him as 'the Australian' and confirm his position alongside Lepage and another athlete named 'Erm' for good points.

Black Athlete in Pink Singlet.description: A Black athlete, identified by commentators as Owens-Delermé, is seen in a pink and white striped singlet with a gold chain and earring, bending forward, hands on knees, catching his breath before the race, with a focused expression; he is noted to be running in lane seven and expected to push the pace.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of three athletes in starting blocks for the 110 Metres Hurdles Decathlon, featuring the Canadian Lepage in lane 5, the Australian Moloney in lane 2, and the Puerto Rican Owens-Delermé in lane 1, all in their national singlets. A male commentator anticipates the race, describing one competitor, later identified as Kevin Mayer, as a "fierce competitor" who "limped his way through the NCAA championships" but "kept showing up" and "scoring personal bests." The camera then pans to a wider view, showing the full starting line with seven athletes, including Garland in lane 7 and Dubler in lane 6, under a bright sun with the stadium's distinctive arched roof and TDK and QNB banners visible. The starting gun fires, and the athletes explode from the blocks, clearing the first hurdle in unison. The commentator notes Kevin Mayer's initial performance, stating it "wouldn't be a super strength" for him, and that he "clipped a hurdle" in the middle. Lepage takes an early lead, followed closely by Aiden Owens-Delermé, who is described as "putting pressure on the Canadian on the outside," and Mayer. The race intensifies as they approach the final hurdles, with Lepage maintaining his advantage. At the finish line, Pierce Lepage crosses first with a time of 13.80 seconds, as shown on the on-screen timer, prompting the commentator to exclaim, "And it is Pierce Lepage who comes through for the win there and he liked that," followed by a "big cheer from the tall Canadian." The unofficial results graphic appears, listing Lepage in 1st, Owens-Delermé in 2nd (+0.11), Mayer in 3rd (+0.13), Golubovic in 4th (+0.14), Garland in 5th (+0.38), and Dubler in 6th (+0.43). Post-race, Lepage is seen walking, adjusting his sunglasses, and waving to the cheering stadium crowd, while Owens-Delermé raises his arms in celebration and Mayer walks with a composed demeanor. The commentator continues to analyze Lepage's performance, noting it's a "humongous personal best" if ratified, comparing it to his previous bests of 14.05 and 14.28, and reflecting on his past DNF in Gotzis, questioning his vulnerability. The stadium is filled with spectators in green and purple stands, cheering under the clear blue sky, with 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' banners prominently displayed along the trackside, and the wind speed noted as +1.1 m/s on the screen, with the Seiko timing system visible at the finish line.

The segment opens with French athlete Kevin Mayer, in his white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet, sitting on the red track, visibly fatigued and adjusting his bright yellow running shoes. An 'UNOFFICIAL RESULTS' graphic for the 110 Metres Hurdles Decathlon shows him in third place, +0.13 behind Lepage, a 'good third place' as noted by the female commentator who observes that Mayer 'looked really, really good so far in this decathlon' despite having 'heavy strapping back on his right ankle,' which he 'got rid of... for the shot put.' She later comments that she hasn't 'quite seen Kevin Mayer come to life yet,' noting he looked 'a bit more energetic in the high jump' but might be experiencing 'nerves' or 'slight apathy.' Mayer then stands and walks toward the discus throw area, transitioning to the next event. The scene shifts to the discus throw circle, where Mayer, now in a focused stance, prepares for his throw. He executes a powerful, spinning motion, releasing the discus with force. The commentator's tone shifts to excitement, exclaiming, 'Oh, that's better! That's what we want from the Frenchman!' as the camera follows the discus’s flight, showing it landing beyond the 50-meter mark. She confirms, 'that discus just bouncing over the 50-meter line,' while a scoreboard graphic indicates '969 PTS' for the throw. Mayer reacts with a raised fist, celebrating his performance, prompting the commentator to encourage him, reminding him he's 'the world record holder,' 'Olympic champion,' and 'silver medal winner in Tokyo last year,' and 'the best man left standing,' concluding that 'that's the fight we wanted to see from Kevin Mayer.' The video then cuts to a slow-motion replay from a low-angle perspective, capturing the full rotation and release of the discus, emphasizing his technique and power. The bright sun casts long shadows across the stadium’s green and purple stands, where spectators contribute to the general ambiance and competitive atmosphere, with officials and media personnel observing from the sidelines, all reinforcing the intensity of the World Athletics Championships Oregon22.

The segment opens under a bright, clear sky at the World Athletics Championships Oregon22, capturing the discus throw event of the decathlon. Mayer, the French athlete in a white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet, executes a powerful discus throw from the circular throwing area. He completes his spin and release, then raises his arms in celebration as a male commentator notes, "And this first bit of emotion that we certainly got from day two and he's just inside that 50-meter line, 49.44." The scoreboard updates to confirm his throw of 49.44m, placing him first in Group A. The male commentator praises Mayer's "outstanding start" and "passion," then shifts focus, stating, "Ziemek needs a couple of more meters," a sentiment echoed by a female commentator. The camera then shifts to Ziemek, the American athlete in a red and blue striped 'USA' singlet, who walks into the throwing circle with a focused expression. The female commentator explains, "Ziemek is just 140 points adrift of Laqash," before exclaiming, "Oh, that's better," as he performs a smooth, controlled spin and releases the discus, which lands beyond the 50-meter mark. She continues, noting that if he "didn't manage to find a few more meters here in the discus... that 140-point gap to the silver medal would just get bigger," emphasizing that "Zach Ziemek really did have to find something a bit more special if he wanted to keep himself in a bronze medal position." The female commentator concludes by discussing "projected scores," stating that "Zach Ziemek holding on to the bronze medal, but only by 10 points ahead of the defending champion," as the large stadium with green and purple seating, sponsor branding (TBS, Nielsen, Seiko), officials in white uniforms, and 'WORLD ATHLETICS CHAMPIONSHIPS OREGON22' banners are visible in the background.

The camera follows the javelin’s flight as it lands deep in the field, and the scoreboard displays his result as 56.92m, placing him sixth. The segment concludes with Lepage walking away, adjusting his sunglasses, as the scoreboard updates with final Group A standings: Mayer in first with 70.31m, Kazmirek in second with 62.52m, and Ziemek in third with 62.18m.

event_blocks.summary: A javelin soars through the sky before landing, prompting officials to measure the throw. Pierce Lepage, wearing a 'CANADA' singlet and a knee brace, walked confidently on the track. The scene shifted to a wide view of the packed stadium with a scoreboard displaying decathlon standings, then focused on the 1500m decathlon start line. A starting lineup graphic detailed competitors. The narrative then shifted to the 1500 metres decathlon, Group 1, with athletes poised at the starting line amidst crowd noise. Commentators highlighted Zach Ziemek as having a good chance for bronze with a time just under 4:40, Lyndon Victor of Grenada as being behind with a slower 1500m PB, and Maicel Uibo of Estonia, currently fifth. Ayden Owens-Delermé of Puerto Rico was noted for his 4:13 1500m time, with potential to gain ground. Defending champion Niklas Kaul of Germany and Cedric Dubler of Australia were mentioned, as were Garland and Neugebauer of the USA and Germany respectively. Sykora of the Czech Republic was acknowledged for making the starting lineup. The stadium's green and purple stands were filled with spectators, and trackside branding included Xfinity, TDK, and World Athletics.

event_blocks.summary: The 1500 metres decathlon at the World Athletics Championships Oregon22 commenced with a close-up of the starting lineup, featuring athletes like Kazmirek, Skotheim, and Golubovic, with commentators highlighting Mullings' hurdles performance and Bastien's late entry, while noting Nowak was running for pride. Kevin Mayer of France, the 2017 champion, was positioned as the favorite for gold. A starting gun signaled the race, with Nowak and others leading initially, but Mayer, noted as a strong 1500m runner, held an 18-second lead over Lepage, with Owens-Delermé also mentioned. As the race progressed, on-screen graphics showed Mayer predicted to finish first, with Lepage, Ziemek, and Owens-Delermé vying for subsequent positions. The competition intensified with '3 LAPS TO GO' and '2 LAPS TO GO' indicators, commentators recalling Mayer's past struggles, including a pole vault clearance, and his determination. The final lap saw Mayer slightly ahead of Lepage and Ziemek, with the stadium crowd cheering intensely. Mayer crossed the finish line first, securing his second world title, collapsing in exhaustion, while Lepage and Ziemek followed. Post-race, Lepage was seen recovering, Mayer celebrated his victory and seasonal best of 8,816 points despite an Achilles injury, and Owens-Delermé celebrated a potential national record and bronze medal. Officials presented Mayer with the gold medal, Ziemek with bronze, and Kazmirek was also present, with on-screen graphics confirming Mayer's 1st place (8816 points) and Ziemek's 3rd place (8676 points), underscoring Ziemek's personal best performance.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up pan across the starting lineup for the 1500 metres decathlon, Group 1, at the World Athletics Championships Oregon 22. A commentator introduces the athletes, mentioning 'Kazmarey Erm,' 'Skoythem,' and 'Kyle Golubovic of Australia.' The camera captures their focused expressions and national singlets, including Skotheim in blue and red 'NORWAY', Golubovic in green and yellow 'AUSTRALIA', Mullings in turquoise 'BAHAMAS', Bastien in red and blue 'USA', and Nowak in yellow and black 'GERMANY'. The commentator notes 'Mullings of Bahamas' has 'done really well in the hurdles' and that 'Bastien was the late call up.' He also mentions 'Novak' didn't perform well in previous attempts, so 'he's running for pride.' The scene shifts to a wider view of the track, showing the full starting line, including Mayer (France) in a white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet. The commentator emphasizes that 'Mayer is running for gold,' calling him 'the 2017 champion, desperate to become a multiple world champion in decathlon,' and stating it's 'his to lose.' A loud starting gun signals the race's beginning, followed by crowd cheers and an excited 'Let's go!' as athletes explode from the blocks, led by Nowak and others. The camera follows the tight pack as they navigate the first curve, with on-screen timing updating to 6.2, 12.3, and 18.3 seconds. The commentator confirms 'Kevin Mayer is a good 1500-meter runner' and has an '18-second lead over Pierce LePage of Canada,' suggesting the 'gold medal is pretty safe in his hands.' On-screen graphics update with 'PREDICTED FINISH POSITION' showing Mayer in 1st. The commentator also mentions 'Aiden Owens-Delerme' who has run 4:13, as the crowd in the green and purple stands watches intently under bright, natural lighting. The athletes maintain fluid, determined movements on the vibrant red track as they approach the first lap’s end, promising an 'interesting and exciting race.'

The 1500m decathlon at the World Athletics Championships Oregon 22 unfolds with a dynamic, tightly contested race, captured from a wide-angle view as runners navigate the first curve. The on-screen 'PREDICTED FINISH POSITION' graphic initially shows Kevin Mayer (France) in first place with 8826 points, followed by Lepage (Canada) at 8738, Ziemek (USA) at 8735, and Aiden Owens-Delermé (Puerto Rico) at 8678. As the race progresses and the camera zooms in, athletes like Mayer in his white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet, Lepage in his red and white striped 'CANADA' singlet with a black knee brace, and Ziemek in his red and blue 'USA' singlet maintain a tight formation. The commentator notes that "Kevin Mayer can go over 8800" points, while observing Ziemek and Lepage running together, separated by "just a few points." The stadium is packed with cheering spectators, their flags waving, contributing to the vibrant atmosphere, underscored by constant crowd noise. The race timer advances from 28.3 to 1:25.1, and the predicted standings fluctuate, with Mayer’s lead narrowing slightly as Lepage and Ziemek gain points. The commentator highlights Aiden Owens-Delermé, noting he's "up front" and "can move up quite a bit in the standings," even stating he "leads by at least 20 meters already," despite the visual of a tight overall pack. Mullings (Bahamas), Nowak (Germany), and Bastien (USA) are also visible in the front group. The '3 LAPS TO GO' graphic appears, signaling the race's progression, as athletes push with determined expressions and powerful strides. The commentator praises Kevin Mayer, stating that "when he gets it right, he's absolutely irresistible in these brutal two-day competitions," recalling his "key, key moment" of needing three attempts to clear 5 meters in the pole vault and showing "a lot of emotion" upon success. Along the track, 'QNB' and 'SEIKO' banners, digital timing equipment, and yellow Seiko boxes are visible, reinforcing the professional setting.

The 1500m decathlon event at the World Athletics Championships Oregon 22 unfolds on a red track under bright daylight, capturing a dynamic segment with a tightly packed group of athletes. The camera alternates between close-up tracking shots and wide-angle views, highlighting the intense competition as the on-screen timer progresses from 1:27.1 to 2:28.2. Commentators discuss an athlete, likely Mayer who is visually leading the PREDICTED FINISH POSITION graphic (8812–8815 points), noting his 'different attitude' and how he came into the championships with 'a lot of question marks' after not competing much in the outdoor season. They recall his silver medal in Tokyo, his self-coaching in field events, and how he managed an Achilles injury, now 'vying for another gold medal.' The commentators excitedly declare he has 'one and a half hands on that gold medal,' as he gets 'closer.' Visually, Lepage, in his red and white 'CANADA' singlet with a black knee brace, and Ziemek, in his red and blue 'USA' singlet, are prominent in the front group, their positions constantly swapping on the graphic, which shows Lepage (8718–8715) and Ziemek (8708–8703) in second and third. The commentators highlight this dynamic, stating 'Lepage is trying to pull away from Zach Ziemek,' and noting that while 'Ziemek's PB is around about seven or eight seconds slower,' Ziemek is currently 'in front of Lepage,' explaining the graphic's fluctuations. Good running is also praised for Mullings, in his turquoise 'BAHAMAS' singlet, alongside Cedric Dubler, in his green and yellow 'AUSTRALIA' singlet. The stadium is packed with cheering spectators in green and purple stands, under a clear blue sky, with prominent trackside branding from TDK, Seiko, and Pinsent Masons, and '2 LAPS TO GO' indicators visible.

The World Athletics Championships Oregon 22 unfolds under bright sunlight, captured by a wide aerial view of a packed stadium. The camera pans across the red track, where athletes are tightly grouped in the 1500m decathlon race. An on-screen graphic displays 'PREDICTED FINISH POSITION,' showing Mayer (France) leading, followed by Lepage (Canada), Ziemek (USA), and Owens-Delermé (Puerto Rico), with points fluctuating in real-time. As the race continues, the camera zooms in on the front pack, showing Mayer in his white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet and Lepage in his red and white 'CANADA' singlet with a black knee brace, jostling for position. The video captures the dynamic and intense final stretch of the 1500m decathlon at the World Athletics Championships Oregon22, unfolding on a vibrant red track with white lane markings, as the race timer in the bottom right corner counts from 3:24.71 to 4:13.02. The stadium crowd, seated in green and purple stands, is going wild and cheering loudly on the back straight, actively responding to the 'cracking battle' for the individual win. Kevin Mayer (France), in a white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet, maintains a strong lead with a focused expression, now just 400 meters away from securing his second world title in the decathlon. Close behind, Pierce Lepage (Canada), in a red and white striped 'CANADA' singlet with a black knee brace on his right leg, is in close pursuit, visibly gaining ground. Zach Ziemek (USA), in a red and blue 'USA' singlet, runs steadily in the front pack, trying to get away from Lepage, while commentators note the crowd is also cheering for the American and Canadian. Aiden Owens-Delermé (Puerto Rico), in a red and blue 'PUERTO RICO' singlet, runs with determination, with commentators highlighting his excitement to represent Puerto Rico and celebrating his second event win of these championships. As the athletes round the final bend and sprint toward the finish line, the camera shifts from close-ups to wide shots, capturing the drama. The final moments of the 1500 metres decathlon at the World Athletics Championships Oregon22 unfold on a red track with white lane lines, as athletes push through intense physical exertion. A wide-angle view shows Mayer (France) leading the pack, with Lepage (Canada), Ziemek (USA), and Owens-Delermé (Puerto Rico) following, their positions and points fluctuating on the 'PREDICTED FINISH POSITION' graphic, which shows Mayer in first place with points ranging from 8770 to 8817. The commentator highlights the battles for "silver and bronze medal position" and praises the "fine, fine times for these men," noting they've been "dragged round to excellent performances." As the race timer progresses from 4:33.0 to 4:58.94, Mayer, in his white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet, crosses the finish line first. The commentator exclaims, "Kevin Mayer is back on top of the world," identifying him as the "World Record Holder," "Double Olympic Silver Medalist," and "Champion du Monde." Mayer collapses dramatically onto the track, lying flat on his back with arms and legs splayed wide, visibly exhausted, a "spent, spent force." The camera zooms in on his face, capturing his labored breathing and a moment of relief as he raises a hand in a 'V' for victory gesture before relaxing again, having overcome past injuries to secure this gold medal, a victory the commentator links to a moment of composure during his pole vault performance where he realized, "I can do this," now securing his "two world titles."

The segment opens with Canadian athlete Lepage, wearing a red and white striped 'CANADA' singlet, sitting visibly exhausted on the grass with hands on knees and head bowed, catching his breath, while being attended to by staff. A commentator notes his 'big personal best' score of 8,701. The scene then cuts to French athlete Mayer, in a white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet, lying flat on the red track after a race, arms and legs spread wide in a starfish pose, clearly drained but triumphant, with a smile and a 'V' for victory gesture. Commentators detail Mayer's performance, explaining how he 'battle[d] through each event,' including a 'long jump runway,' despite 'pain in that right Achilles,' and announce his 'seasonal best' score of 8,816. The camera then shifts to Puerto Rican athlete Owens-Delermé, in a red 'PUERTO RICO' singlet, running on the track with mouth open in exertion, then slowing to a walk, raising his arms in celebration, clapping, and high-fiving a person off-screen. Throughout, the background features a large stadium crowd in green and purple stands, cheering and waving, their sounds contributing to the competitive atmosphere, with banners for 'Pinsent Masons' and 'Nielsen' visible along the professional red track. The overall mood is one of intense physical exertion followed by celebration and relief, underscored by the commentators' analysis of the decathlon results and individual achievements.

Visually, Owens-Delermé, in his red 'PUERTO RICO' singlet, is seen walking along the red track, raising his arms in triumph, clapping, and high-fiving others, visibly elated and bending forward to catch his breath, perfectly aligning with the audio's mention of a 'national record for Puerto Rico.' The commentary then shifts to 'a glittering gold for Kevin Mayer,' as Mayer, in his white and blue 'FRANCE' singlet, is shown receiving a gold medal from an official, smiling and adjusting it around his neck. The commentator describes Ziemek as 'a spent force' and confirms his bronze medal, stating, 'now he does get the confirmation, the medal is yours, they're not taking it away. Double Z as you like to call him, will be over the moon.' Visually, Ziemek, in his red and blue 'USA' singlet, receives a bronze medal, placing it around his neck with a proud expression, and embraces Mayer. Kazmirek, in his German singlet, is also present, interacting with Mayer. The stadium is filled with spectators, and the celebratory atmosphere is palpable, with on-screen graphics displaying Mayer in 1st with 8816 points and Ziemek in 3rd with 8676 points. Seiko timing equipment and 'OREGON22' event banners are visible. The commentator concludes by highlighting Ziemek's strong performance, securing a personal best and his best two days of his career, before the segment transitions to a colorful graphic promoting '#WORLDATHLETICSCHAMPS'.
